problems.question: What is the perimeter of a shape?
choices: A) The space inside the shape
B) The distance around the shape

feedback.incorrect: Perimeter is the distance around a shape, not inside it.

hints: Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around the outside.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A rectangle on a grid is 6 units long and 3 units wide. Count all sides. What is the perimeter (in units)?

feedback.incorrect: Add all four sides: 6+3+6+3 = 18 units.

hints: Add up all the side lengths: 6+3+6+3.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A rectangle is 9 cm long and 4 cm wide. What is the perimeter?

feedback.incorrect: Add all four sides: 8+5+8+5 = 26 cm.

hints: Add every side: 8+5+8+5 = ?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A square has a side length of 7 m. What is the perimeter, in m?

feedback.incorrect: A square has 4 sides of 7 m each: 4 × 7 = 28 m.

hints: A square has 4 equal sides: 7 × 4 = ?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A triangle has sides 5 ft, 8 ft, and 11 ft. What is the perimeter?

feedback.incorrect: P = 2 × (length + width) = 2 × 12 = 24 ft.

hints: Add length+width then double, or add all 4 sides.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A triangle has a perimeter of 25 cm. Two sides are 9 cm and 8 cm. What is the third side, in cm?

feedback.incorrect: 25 − 9 − 8 = 8 cm for the third side.

hints: Total perimeter − known sides = unknown side.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A rectangle has a perimeter of 30 m and a length of 9 m. What is the width?
hints: P = 2(L+W). Find L+W first.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A square has a perimeter of 56 cm. What is the side length, in cm?

feedback.incorrect: All 4 sides equal: 56 ÷ 4 = 14 cm.

hints: Square sides equal — divide perimeter by 4.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Which unit measures area?

feedback.correct: Right — area is in square units.
feedback.incorrect: Area is measured in square units like sq m or sq cm.

hints: Area uses square units (sq m, sq cm, sq ft).
problems.feedback_correct: Right — area is in square units.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A rectangle is 8 cm long and 5 cm wide. What is the perimeter, in cm?

feedback.incorrect: Perimeter = 2 × (8 + 5) = 26 cm.

hints: P = 2 × (length + width).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Rectangle A is 7×2. Rectangle B is 5×4. Which statement is true?

feedback.incorrect: Different rectangles can have the same perimeter but different areas.

hints: Same perimeter doesn't always mean same area.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Three rectangles all have perimeter 20 cm: A(9×1), B(7×3), C(5×5). Which has the greatest area?

feedback.incorrect: A square 5×5 has the largest area for perimeter 20.

hints: A square gives the most area for a given perimeter.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Two rectangles have perimeter 24 cm: A is 10×2, B is 6×6. How much more is B's area than A's, in sq cm?

feedback.correct: Right! B's area (36) − A's area (20) = 16 sq cm.
feedback.incorrect: B is 6×6 = 36 sq cm. A is 10×2 = 20 sq cm. Difference = 16 sq cm.

hints: B is 6×6 = 36 sq cm. A is 10×2 = 20 sq cm. B has 16 more.
problems.feedback_correct: Right! B's area (36) − A's area (20) = 16 sq cm.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Three rectangles all have area 24 sq cm: A(24×1), B(8×3), C(6×4). Which has the smallest perimeter?

feedback.incorrect: A square 5×5 has the smallest perimeter for area 25.

hints: A square uses the least fence for a given area.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Two rectangles have area 36 sq cm: A is 18×2, B is 6×6. How much more is A's perimeter than B's, in cm?

feedback.correct: Yes! A's perimeter (40) − B's perimeter (24) = 16 cm.
feedback.incorrect: A: P = 2(18+2) = 40 cm. B: P = 2(6+6) = 24 cm. Difference = 16 cm.

problems.feedback_correct: Yes! A's perimeter (40) − B's perimeter (24) = 16 cm.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A rectangular playground is 15 m long and 8 m wide. How much fencing is needed to go around it?
hints: Add length+width and double: 2 × (15+8).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A rectangular garden has a perimeter of 38 ft and a width of 7 ft. What is the length?
hints: Subtract two widths from the perimeter, then halve.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Tina has 28 m of fence for a rectangular pen with the greatest area. What is the area, in sq m?

feedback.incorrect: Side = 28 ÷ 4 = 7 m. Area = 7 × 7 = 49 sq m.

hints: A square gives the most area: side = perim ÷ 4.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A field needs area 40 sq m. Options: 40×1, 10×4, 8×5. Which option uses the least fencing? Give the perimeter, in m.

feedback.incorrect: 8 × 5 has the smallest perimeter: P = 2(8+5) = 26 m.

hints: Compare perimeters: 40+1=41, 10+4=14, 8+5=13. Pick smallest.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: A rectangle has perimeter 22 cm and area 24 sq cm. What is the longer side, in cm?

feedback.incorrect: 8 + 3 = 11 and 8 × 3 = 24. The longer side is 8 cm.

hints: L+W = 11 and L×W = 24. Try pairs that multiply to 24.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.10 Lesson — U10_perimeter/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: An irregular hexagon has a perimeter of 42 cm. Five sides measure 6, 8, 7, 9, and 5 cm. What is the sixth side? Explain.
pretest.correct_answer: Sixth side = 7 cm. Add known sides: 6+8+7+9+5 = 35. Missing = 42 − 35 = 7 cm.
hints: Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.

feedback.correct: Right! The answer is Sixth side = 7 cm. Add known sides: 6+8+7+9+5 = 35. Missing = 42 − 35 = 7 cm.
feedback.incorrect: The answer is Sixth side = 7 cm. Add known sides: 6+8+7+9+5 = 35. Missing = 42 − 35 = 7 cm. Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.

pretest.question: A square and a rectangle both have a perimeter of 24 cm. The rectangle is 8 cm long. What is the side length of the square? What is the width of the rectangle?
pretest.correct_answer: Square side = 6 cm (24 ÷ 4 = 6). Rectangle width: (8 + w) × 2 = 24 → 8 + w = 12 → w = 4 cm.
hints: Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.

feedback.correct: Yes! The answer is Square side = 6 cm (24 ÷ 4 = 6). Rectangle width: (8 + w) × 2 = 24 → 8 + w = 12 → w = 4 cm.

pretest.question: Why does the square always have the greatest area among rectangles with the same perimeter? Explain using an example.
pretest.correct_answer: The square has sides as equal as possible, making it most compact. Example P=16: 7×1=7, 6×2=12, 5×3=15, 4×4=16. As sides get closer to equal, area increases.
hints: Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.

feedback.correct: Yes! The answer is The square has sides as equal as possible, making it most compact. Example P=16: 7×1=7, 6×2=12, 5×3=15, 4×4=16. As sides get closer to equal, area increases.
feedback.incorrect: The answer is The square has sides as equal as possible, making it most compact. Example P=16: 7×1=7, 6×2=12, 5×3=15, 4×4=16. As sides get closer to equal, area increases. Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.

pretest.question: Explain in your own words why perimeter and area are different. Why can two shapes have the same perimeter but different areas?
pretest.correct_answer: Perimeter measures distance around (linear). Area measures space inside (square units). Shapes can be stretched differently — a long thin rectangle and a squarish rectangle can have the same perimeter but different amounts of space inside.
hints: Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.

feedback.correct: Great! The answer is Perimeter measures distance around (linear). Area measures space inside (square units). Shapes can be stretched differently — a long thin rectangle and a squarish rectangle can have the same perimeter but different amounts of space inside.
feedback.incorrect: The answer is Perimeter measures distance around (linear). Area measures space inside (square units). Shapes can be stretched differently — a long thin rectangle and a squarish rectangle can have the same perimeter but different amounts of space inside. Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.

pretest.question: List ALL whole-number rectangles with area 24 sq cm. Calculate the perimeter of each. Which has the smallest perimeter?
hints: Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.

feedback.correct: Yes! The answer is Factor pairs: (24,1)→P=50, (12,2)→P=28, (8,3)→P=22, (6,4)→P=20. Smallest: 6×4=20 cm.

pretest.question: Find two different rectangles that each have an area of 36 sq cm. Calculate the perimeter of each. Which has the smaller perimeter?
pretest.correct_answer: Example: 9×4 → Area=36, P=26. 6×6 → Area=36, P=24. The square (6×6) has the smaller perimeter.
hints: Perimeter is the distance ALL the way around. Add every side.
